serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of traditional kaya toast consists of two thin slices of toasted white bread spread with kaya and topped with cold butter slices, weighing approximately 80 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Kaya Toast — HPB Singapore Foodo / Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo lists kaya toast as a standard kopitiam item. Per-100g values are derived from HPB's published nutrient composition for kaya toast (toasted white bread with kaya spread and butter), cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entries for white bread (NDB 18069), butter (NDB 01001), and kaya (coconut egg jam) component estimates from FAO/INFOODS SEA data. The composite per-100g profile reflects approximately 60% toasted white bread, 25% kaya, and 15% butter by weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kaya toast as a composite dish. GI of 70 is assigned based on the dominant component: white bread (GI 70–75, glucose reference) as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). Kaya (coconut jam) contributes additional sugar but the bread matrix dominates the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 38.0) / 100 = 26.6, using 80 g serving with 47.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g = 38.0 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (310 mg per 100 g; approximately 248 mg per 80 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit kaya toast to occasional consumption and avoid pairing with other high-sodium kopitiam items such as soft-boiled eggs with soy sauce, as the combined sodium load can approach 400–500 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 70) and high glycemic load per serving (GL 26.6) driven by refined white bread and added sugar in kaya
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should treat kaya toast as an occasional item; substituting wholegrain or lower-GI bread and reducing kaya portion size can meaningfully lower the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content (6.8 g per 100 g) from butter and coconut-based kaya, contributing approximately 5.4 g saturated fat per 80 g serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit kaya toast frequency and consider requesting reduced butter to lower saturated fat intake, keeping total saturated fat within the recommended ceiling of less than 10% of daily energy.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Traditional Kaya Toast is a quintessential Singaporean kopitiam (coffee shop) breakfast dish consisting of two thin slices of white bread that are toasted until crisp and golden, then generously spread with kaya — a fragrant, sweet coconut and egg jam flavoured with pandan leaves — and topped with thin slices of cold salted butter. The contrast between the warm, crunchy toast and the cold, melting butter is a defining textural feature. Kaya itself is made by slow-cooking coconut milk, eggs, sugar, and pandan extract, resulting in a smooth, custard-like spread with a distinctive green or golden-brown colour depending on the recipe. The dish is deeply embedded in Singaporean and Malaysian Hainanese immigrant food culture, originating from early 20th-century Hainanese coffeehouse workers who adapted Western toast to local ingredients. It is traditionally served alongside soft-boiled eggs seasoned with dark soy sauce and white pepper, and a cup of kopi (local coffee) or teh (tea). Nutritionally, kaya toast is a moderate-energy food (~320 kcal per 100 g) with a high glycemic index (GI 70) due to refined white bread and added sugar in kaya, making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. The butter component contributes saturated fat (~6.8 g per 100 g), which is relevant for cardiovascular risk management. Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for hypertensive individuals. It provides modest protein (~6.2 g per 100 g) and limited dietary fibre (~1.5 g per 100 g). The dish is vegetarian and halal-certified in most kopitiam settings.
common_user_queries: How many calories in kaya toast?
Is kaya toast suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of kaya toast?
What is the difference between kaya toast and regular toast?
Can I eat kaya toast if I have high blood pressure?
What is a traditional Singapore breakfast?

confidence_reason: HPB Foodo lists kaya toast as a standard kopitiam item and provides a nutrient profile. Per-100g values are adapted from HPB data cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy component data for white bread and butter, and FAO/INFOODS SEA estimates for kaya. No single exact-match laboratory analysis record was available for the specific Toast Box formulation; therefore confidence is medium rather than high.
notes: Vitamin C (vitamin_c_mg) is 0.0; this is confirmed zero as kaya toast contains no vitamin-C-bearing ingredients (no fruit, no vegetables). This is naturally zero for this food. All other -1 sentinel values indicate nutrients not reported in the primary HPB/USDA composite source for this food item: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are included as best-available estimates rather than -1 because the composite source provides reasonable component-level data. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for kaya toast as a composite dish was found in the approved GI literature. Serving size of 80 g (2 slices with kaya and butter) is based on standard kopitiam portion as described in HPB Foodo portion guidance. The halal status reflects standard kopitiam practice; individual restaurant certification should be verified. Toast Box - Funan is the source restaurant per field survey input record.
